title: The Color Yellow

jokes.text: I'm blowing up the stump, not the school.
jokes.setup: Willie talking about explosives and blowing up detention hall
jokes.punchline: Clarification that he's just removing a tree stump, not destroying school property
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection joke with good character voice for Willie. The art room comment adds nice character detail.

jokes.text: Wait, here comes the 'mykeeyah.' What's a mykeeyah? My Kia!
jokes.setup: Willie warns about an incoming 'mykeeyah' sound
jokes.punchline: It's actually Homer's car (Kia) being destroyed, with accent-based wordplay
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that uses Willie's accent to set up the pun. Good economy and quotable.

jokes.text: I'm laughing to fit in, but I still don't get it.
jokes.setup: Homer and Willie laughing at the Kia joke
jokes.punchline: Homer admits he doesn't understand but is fake-laughing anyway
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - his social insecurity and honesty about not getting jokes is very character-specific.

jokes.text: Miss Hoover, what's our lesson today? Is it school?
jokes.setup: Student asking teacher about the day's lesson
jokes.punchline: Ralph's nonsensical follow-up question
jokes.explanation: Classic Ralph Wiggum non-sequitur. Perfect character voice but relies on established 'Ralph is dumb' formula.

jokes.text: horse thieves, deadbeats, horse beats, dead thieves
jokes.setup: Homer listing bad Simpson ancestors
jokes.punchline: The word scrambling creates increasingly absurd combinations
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that gets funnier as it goes. Good rhythm and escalation.

jokes.text: (whispering): ...alcoholics.
jokes.setup: Homer listing terrible ancestors
jokes.punchline: Whispering 'alcoholics' as if it's the worst one, despite Homer's own drinking
jokes.explanation: Excellent character irony. Homer's shame about alcoholism while being an alcoholic himself is perfectly hypocritical.

jokes.text: Maybe the nicest was Abigail Simpson, who you know as the Pittsburgh Poisoner.
jokes.setup: Grampa offering hope about a 'nice' ancestor
jokes.punchline: The 'nice' one is actually a serial poisoner
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark comedy misdirection. The casual delivery of horrifying information is expertly crafted.

jokes.text: Adam and Eve Simpson, or as you may know them, Julius and Ethel Rosenberg.
jokes.setup: Lisa mentions going back to Adam and Eve for good ancestors
jokes.punchline: Grampa claims they're actually the executed communist spies
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist escalation. The biblical reference twisted into Cold War history is genuinely surprising and dark.

jokes.text: (gasps) So dusty.
jokes.setup: Lisa looking through old items in the attic
jokes.punchline: Exaggerated reaction to dustiness, implying a big dust cloud
jokes.explanation: Basic visual gag reaction. Functional but unremarkable - standard cartoon dusty attic bit.

jokes.text: ...because tomorrow I shall get my very first slave. (all gasp) I can't believe we're descended from slave owners. Me neither. For once, the Simpsons were in management.
jokes.setup: Family horrified to discover they descended from slave owners
jokes.punchline: Homer's twisted pride that the family was finally in a management position
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer response - completely missing the moral horror and focusing on the 'business success' angle

jokes.text: For once, the Simpsons were in management.
jokes.setup: Family learning they're descended from slave owners
jokes.punchline: Homer being proud they were finally in charge of something
jokes.explanation: Dark but brilliant character moment. Homer's complete moral blindness and focus on status is perfectly captured.

jokes.text: What's for dinner? Possum. Oh, can't we have opossum? Not on your salary.
jokes.setup: Period-appropriate dinner conversation
jokes.punchline: Treating possum vs opossum as a class distinction based on salary
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that treats identical animals as different social classes. Good period comedy.

jokes.text: I can't believe I buttoned my britches for this.
jokes.setup: Hiram disappointed by dinner
jokes.punchline: Period-appropriate expression of disgust
jokes.explanation: Good period-specific language joke. The formal phrasing for a mundane complaint works well.

jokes.text: Then they should've called it the Aboveground Normal-Road.
jokes.setup: Marge explaining the Underground Railroad wasn't actually underground
jokes.punchline: Bart's literal-minded alternative name
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart logic - literal-minded and missing the point but in a clever way. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Good point, Bart. Absolutely. Hadn't thought of that before. Very good, Bart.
jokes.setup: Bart making his Underground Railroad observation
jokes.punchline: Homer treating it as genuinely insightful
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Homer's lack of historical understanding and tendency to praise obvious observations.

jokes.text: It's like when your father and I left the movie Carrie right after she was crowned prom queen. She was so happy. She had a lot of problems, but they were all behind her.
jokes.setup: Marge explaining why they should quit while ahead
jokes.punchline: Missing the entire horrific climax of Carrie by leaving early
jokes.explanation: Brilliant ironic reference that works perfectly for Marge's naive optimism. Anyone who knows Carrie gets the dark humor.

jokes.text: I don't like this new Viennese dance craze, 'the waltz.' One, two, three, one, two, three... Where's the 'four'? All music needs a four!
jokes.setup: Period ball scene with waltz music
jokes.punchline: Colonel Burns complaining about 3/4 time signature
jokes.explanation: Clever musical humor that shows Burns as curmudgeonly about everything, even mathematical precision in music.

jokes.text: Actually, I'm here to help you. Follow me north to freedom. I don't think so. Why? Because I'm young and I'm a woman? No, because you're pointing south.
jokes.setup: Eliza trying to rescue Virgil
jokes.punchline: Her incompetence rather than prejudice being the issue
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection joke that subverts expectations about period prejudices with simple incompetence.

jokes.text: (sheepish chuckle) Oh, right, right. Sorry. It's my first time. That's all right. It's my 14th.
jokes.setup: Eliza's directional mistake
jokes.punchline: Virgil being an experienced escape veteran
jokes.explanation: Good character dynamic establishing Virgil as more competent than his would-be rescuer.

jokes.text: After them! Obviously. Well, you didn't say it.
jokes.setup: Chase scene with slave catchers
jokes.punchline: Literal-minded response to obvious command
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective bit about literal-mindedness during action scene. Breaks tension appropriately.

jokes.text: Bart, can you believe we're married to those nerds? (both laugh)
jokes.setup: Marge and Lisa going to library for research
jokes.punchline: Homer and Bart bonding over their wives/family being intellectual
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer and Bart's shared anti-intellectual bond. Bart being 'married' to Lisa is a nice touch.

jokes.text: Rule number one: never be better than me. And I'm pretty bad.
jokes.setup: Clown teaching Eliza to perform
jokes.punchline: Self-aware admission of terrible skill level
jokes.explanation: Good self-deprecating humor that establishes character and sets low expectations for the performance.

jokes.text: Why do I always ask for a nickel? That's like 20 bucks in 2010 money.
jokes.setup: Clown asking for nickel to see bearded lady
jokes.punchline: Self-aware anachronistic comment about inflation
jokes.explanation: Meta joke breaking the fourth wall about historical economics. Clever but breaks character immersion slightly.

jokes.text: Maybe you'll change your mind about me after you try some of my wheel cakes. Wheel cakes? They're just honey-sweetened batter... Go on. ...deep-fried in hog lard. Lard, you say. Then dusted with powdered sugar. (muffled): You can stay.
jokes.setup: Mabel trying to convince Hiram to help with food
jokes.punchline: Each ingredient makes him more interested, completely sold by sugar
jokes.explanation: Good escalating seduction through increasingly unhealthy food descriptions

jokes.text: Martin Luther King had a dream. Dreams are where Elmo and Toy Story had a party and I went there. Yay. My turn is over.
jokes.setup: Ralph giving Black History Month presentation
jokes.punchline: Completely misunderstanding the MLK reference
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph moment - completely missing profound historical significance for childish fantasy. Very characteristic.

jokes.text: No, no, no, not now! Quit, quit! No, don't quit the program, just the update, you stupid... (awkward chuckle) Computers, right?
jokes.setup: Lisa's presentation having technical difficulties
jokes.punchline: Computer malfunction during important presentation with awkward recovery
jokes.explanation: Standard tech malfunction humor. Relatable but not particularly original or crafted.

jokes.text: I hold in my hand another diary. That of my great-great-great... (takes hit from inhaler) ...great-great grandfafa, Milford van Houten.
jokes.setup: Milhouse dramatically revealing counter-evidence
jokes.punchline: Needing inhaler mid-dramatic speech and saying 'grandfafa'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Milhouse moment - trying to be dramatic but undermined by his physical weakness and childish speech.

jokes.text: I would say your price is... a pleasant surprise. That's exactly my price.
jokes.setup: Burns negotiating to find Hiram's price
jokes.punchline: Vague 'pleasant surprise' being exactly what he wants
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing both men's weakness - Burns' manipulation and Hiram's easy corruptibility.

jokes.text: From the day you were born, you were brought up to crave the approval of a man. And I know you really want to be a good girl, and good girls are seen and not heard.
jokes.setup: Burns confronting Eliza about interfering
jokes.punchline: Psychological manipulation using period gender expectations
jokes.explanation: Dark but clever social commentary wrapped in period-appropriate villainy. Shows Burns' manipulative intelligence.

jokes.text: Your surprise is a new pair of shoes. Oh, my God, that surprise is so pleasant.
jokes.setup: Hiram revealing Virgil's location for his 'pleasant surprise'
jokes.punchline: The pathetically small reward of shoes
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows how easily Hiram was bought with something trivial. The enthusiasm makes it funnier.

jokes.text: It didn't help that the next day, he drank bad well water and went blind.
jokes.setup: Milhouse's ancestor couldn't look at Eliza again
jokes.punchline: Literal blindness rather than metaphorical shame
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy escalation. Takes metaphorical shame and makes it literally medical in a perfectly absurd way.

jokes.text: And so many Obamas. (all impersonating Obama) Yes, we can. Audacity of hope. Let me be clear.
jokes.setup: Discussion of repetitive Black History Month presentations
jokes.punchline: Multiple students doing Obama impressions with his catchphrases
jokes.explanation: Topical humor about oversaturation of Obama references. Feels somewhat dated and not deeply crafted.

jokes.text: We have a film vault? It's where we hooked up during the Christmas party. Oh.
jokes.setup: Surprise about library having a film vault
jokes.punchline: Awkward personal revelation about romantic encounter there
jokes.explanation: Standard awkward oversharing joke. Well-structured but not particularly original or memorable.

jokes.text: I'll take that secret to my grave-- or urn, or medical school dissecting table, or wherever you're dumping me.
jokes.setup: Grampa claiming he'll keep secret to his grave
jokes.punchline: Acknowledging his family's likely disrespect for his remains
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor about elderly neglect, very characteristic of Grampa's pessimistic worldview

jokes.text: D-Day is June 6th. Repeat: June 6th. Allied forces will land on the beaches of Normandy in the following order-- Utah, Omaha, Gold, Juno. Are you writing this down, Fritz?
jokes.setup: Grampa being pressured to reveal family secrets
jokes.punchline: Confusing family history with WWII intelligence
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grampa moment showing his confused mental state and tendency to mix up historical periods. Very character-specific.

jokes.text: Very well. You may have won this round, but someday my history will be told by my descendants Ken and Ric Burns.
jokes.setup: Burns being defeated and making threats
jokes.punchline: Reference to real documentary filmmakers Ken Burns and Ric Burns
jokes.explanation: Clever meta joke connecting fictional Burns family to real documentary filmmakers. Works on multiple levels for different audiences.

jokes.text: You defied your husband for me? Man, this is the '60s. Now let's take you to a place where a black man can blend in-- Canada.
jokes.setup: Emotional moment about defying husband
jokes.punchline: Virgil's casual '60s reference and Canada joke
jokes.explanation: Decent anachronistic humor about Canada's diversity, though somewhat surface-level

jokes.text: Man, this is the '60s. Now let's take you to a place where a black man can blend in-- Canada.
jokes.setup: Mabel helping Virgil escape
jokes.punchline: Calling 1860s the '60s and suggesting Canada as place to blend in
jokes.explanation: Mild anachronism humor and gentle stereotype about Canadian tolerance. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: It makes you look like a potbellied stove. (annoyed grunt) Hey, hey, 'Honest Abe.' (laughs) Oh, I'm a riot.
jokes.setup: Lincoln offering his hat to disguise Mabel's hair
jokes.punchline: Honest assessment followed by self-congratulatory joke about his nickname
jokes.explanation: Good historical character humor showing Lincoln as both honest and slightly self-aware about his reputation.

jokes.text: Mabel she divorced Hiram and got one of his shoes in the settlement.
jokes.setup: Explaining Mabel's new life after divorce
jokes.punchline: Pathetically small divorce settlement of a single shoe
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that completes the shoe running gag while showing the absurdity of divorce settlements in that era.

jokes.text: They got married, and since Virgil had no last name, they both took the name Simpson so that Mabel could keep all her monogrammed table linens.
jokes.setup: Explaining how they got the Simpson name
jokes.punchline: Practical reason being monogrammed linens rather than sentiment
jokes.explanation: Good practical humor about domestic concerns trumping romance

jokes.text: We've regained our family honor, and we're 1/64th black. So that's why I'm so cool. That's why my jazz is so smooth. And that's why I earn less than my white co-workers.
jokes.setup: Family celebrating their newfound heritage
jokes.punchline: Escalating from positive stereotypes to systemic racism
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from silly stereotypes to serious social commentary. The progression from positive to negative is expertly crafted.

jokes.text: Well, it's hard to explain this to a young person, but people of my generation are, you know... Racists? That's it.
jokes.setup: Grampa trying to explain his generation's attitudes
jokes.punchline: Blunt admission of racism after awkward pause
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark comedy moment with Grampa's matter-of-fact admission. The awkward buildup makes the punchline hit harder.

jokes.text: I don't know what the big deal is. I mean, it never bothered any of you that my father's French. You know, Bouvier? So that's why I love drinking so much. I'm French, not you.
jokes.setup: Discussion of mixed heritage
jokes.punchline: Homer claiming French alcoholism stereotype while Marge corrects him
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's eagerness to claim any excuse for his drinking and his confusion about family lineage.

tmdb_overview: When Miss Hoover asks her students to research their family history, Lisa is horrified to discover that most of her ancestors were bad people – a motley crew of horse thieves and deadbeats. But while rummaging through the attic, Lisa happens upon a diary kept by her ancestor, Eliza Simpson. As Eliza’s story unfolds, Lisa learns that her family was part of the Underground Railroad, a group that helped slaves escape to freedom. Eliza recounts liberating a slave named Virgil, but when Lisa presents her findings at school, some of her classmates refute it, leaving Lisa determined to exonerate her family’s name.
